cross_connections.display_label: Hooker descendant served in his Troop of horses, 1757 alarm.

narrative_blocks.header: Chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill
narrative_blocks.body: As chairman of the Glastonbury town meeting he led the formal denunciation of the Boston Port Bill — one of the acts that precipitated the Revolution. He already held a commission as Colonel of a Troop of Horse and would lead that troop into the field.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried home from Long Island on a litter
narrative_blocks.body: Present at the Battle of Long Island in August 1776 at age 67, he fell so ill during the campaign that he was carried back to Glastonbury on a litter. He resigned his commission three months later and never rejoined the Continental Army.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost a Susquehanna land claim in court
narrative_blocks.body: He held substantial Connecticut Susquehanna lands in Pennsylvania — claimed under Connecticut's sea-to-sea royal charter. He lost that investment entirely when the courts ruled against the Connecticut claim.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Ruth brought a large fortune to him
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Ruth Wright brought a substantial fortune to the marriage, inherited from her wealthy father Daniel Wright Jr. They married on his 21st birthday — December 31, 1730 — and son George later served with him in the Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: He lost four sons to black canker
narrative_blocks.body: Of Colonel Elizur Talcott's twelve children with Ruth Wright, four young sons died of what the family record called 'black canker' between 1748 and 1750. He twice gave a dead son's name to a later child, and only a handful of the twelve lived to marry.

military_service.unit: Sixth Connecticut Militia (Sixth Regiment, Colony of Connecticut)
military_service.notes: Colonel of the Sixth Connecticut Militia 1775-1776. At age 67, present at the Battle of Long Island (Aug 27, 1776). Became ill during the campaign and was carried back to Glastonbury CT on a litter; resigned his commission three months later. Surviving documentation: a holograph order signed by Col. Elizur Talcott in Glastonbury CT June 12, 1776 to Capt. Benjamin Wright of the Fourth Company directing election of a new Lieutenant — the document was signed off by Titus Hosmer for the Lower House (Continental Congress delegate and Articles of Confederation signer) and George Wyllys for the Upper House (Connecticut Secretary of State). See DOC013.

Commanded a Troop of horses raised at Glastonbury for service in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry and parts adjacent, August 1757. His unit included Hooker descendant Samuel Hooker Jr (H00228) for 14 days of service. Source: Berlin-Peck Memorial Library — Berlin in the French and Indian War 1753-1763.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker descendant served in his Troop of horses, 1757 alarm.

narrative_blocks.header: Chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill
narrative_blocks.body: As chairman of the Glastonbury town meeting he led the formal denunciation of the Boston Port Bill — one of the acts that precipitated the Revolution. He already held a commission as Colonel of a Troop of Horse and would lead that troop into the field.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried home from Long Island on a litter
narrative_blocks.body: Present at the Battle of Long Island in August 1776 at age 67, he fell so ill during the campaign that he was carried back to Glastonbury on a litter. He resigned his commission three months later and never rejoined the Continental Army.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost a Susquehanna land claim in court
narrative_blocks.body: He held substantial Connecticut Susquehanna lands in Pennsylvania — claimed under Connecticut's sea-to-sea royal charter. He lost that investment entirely when the courts ruled against the Connecticut claim.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Ruth brought a large fortune to him
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Ruth Wright brought a substantial fortune to the marriage, inherited from her wealthy father Daniel Wright Jr. They married on his 21st birthday — December 31, 1730 — and son George later served with him in the Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: He lost four sons to black canker
narrative_blocks.body: Of Colonel Elizur Talcott's twelve children with Ruth Wright, four young sons died of what the family record called 'black canker' between 1748 and 1750. He twice gave a dead son's name to a later child, and only a handful of the twelve lived to marry.

military_service.unit: Sixth Connecticut Militia (Sixth Regiment, Colony of Connecticut)
military_service.notes: Colonel of the Sixth Connecticut Militia 1775-1776. At age 67, present at the Battle of Long Island (Aug 27, 1776). Became ill during the campaign and was carried back to Glastonbury CT on a litter; resigned his commission three months later. Surviving documentation: a holograph order signed by Col. Elizur Talcott in Glastonbury CT June 12, 1776 to Capt. Benjamin Wright of the Fourth Company directing election of a new Lieutenant — the document was signed off by Titus Hosmer for the Lower House (Continental Congress delegate and Articles of Confederation signer) and George Wyllys for the Upper House (Connecticut Secretary of State). See DOC013.

Commanded a Troop of horses raised at Glastonbury for service in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry and parts adjacent, August 1757. His unit included Hooker descendant Samuel Hooker Jr (H00228) for 14 days of service. Source: Berlin-Peck Memorial Library — Berlin in the French and Indian War 1753-1763.

burial.plot_notes: Quarryville Cemetery, Bolton CT. FindAGrave Memorial ID 10706544.

narrative_blocks.header: She brought a fortune to her marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Ruth Wright was an only child who inherited a large fortune from her wealthy father and brought it to her marriage with Elizur Talcott. Family memory described her as a remarkably handsome woman, tall and erect, with golden hair and black eyes.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Bradford of the Mayflower
narrative_blocks.body: George Talcott's first wife, Vienna Bradford, was a direct descendant of Governor William Bradford of the Mayflower. She died in 1785 at age 27. George remarried the following year, had six more children, and lived on the family homestead in Glastonbury — land his family had farmed since his grandfather's 1699 deed.

narrative_blocks.header: At the Long Island retreat with his father
narrative_blocks.body: He served in the Revolution alongside his father Col. Elizur and his brothers, present at the army’s night escape from Long Island. A Glastonbury farmer, he held the homestead that came down through his father.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife traced to the Mayflower
narrative_blocks.body: He married Vienna Bradford in 1777 — of the Plymouth Bradford line — and lost her at twenty-seven. His second marriage, to Abigail Goodrich, lasted until his death from “inflammation of the brain” in 1813.

parents.father_research_notes: Jeremiah Bradford, physician in Connecticut, practiced in Middle Haddam, died 1814. Descendant of Gov. William Bradford through: Gov. William Bradford → William Bradford Jr. (b.1624) → Samuel Bradford (b.1668) → Gershom Bradford (b. Dec 21, 1691) → Jeremiah Bradford (b.1734) → Vienna (b.1757). Gov. William Bradford was governor of Plymouth Colony and arrived on the Mayflower in 1620.

narrative_blocks.header: Mayflower descendant — her ancestor founded Plymouth Colony
narrative_blocks.body: Vienna Bradford was the great-great-great-granddaughter of Governor William Bradford, who arrived on the Mayflower in 1620 and governed Plymouth Colony for thirty years. She died in Glastonbury on August 17, 1785, at age 27, leaving behind her husband George Talcott and several children — including Harriet, Fanny, and Rebecca.

parents.mother_research_notes: Abigail Goodrich (T01018's second wife, daughter of John and Abigail Deming Goodrich).

notable.notable_blurb: Third Chief of U.S. Army Ordnance, 1848-1851

military_service.war: War of 1812 + Mexican-American War + post-1812 Ordnance Corps
military_service.notes:
Career arc:
- 1813: Entered Army from NYC as 3rd Lt of Infantry; quickly promoted 2nd Lt; Aug 1813 transferred to Ordnance duty + 1st Lt.
- Initially commanded New York City Arsenal, then Albany Arsenal.
- May 1814: reverted to 1st Lt; retained on Ordnance duty post-War of 1812.
- Massachusetts: oversaw construction of new arsenal at Watertown.
- Allegheny Arsenal command (near Pittsburgh PA).
- 1821: transferred to 2nd Artillery, detailed to ordnance.
- Aug 1823: Brevet Major (10 years faithful service in one grade).
- Feb 1824 controversy: while at Allegheny Arsenal, ordered horsewhipping of escaped/recaptured deserter; court-martialed twice, both panels found him not guilty.
- Sept 1824 - Jan 1832: commanded Watervliet Arsenal NY.
- May 1832: promoted Lt Col; Inspector of Arsenals and Armories for the Ordnance Corps.
- April 1839: Acting Chief of Ordnance Bureau (under Van Buren administration).
- 1842: appointed Asst Chief of Ordnance (de facto running daily operations for 3 years).
- Mexican-American War: directed expansion of Ordnance Department responsibilities, new ordnance depots, field service operations.
- March 25, 1848: promoted Colonel, became 3rd Chief of Ordnance (after death of Col. George Bomford).
- March 3, 1849: Brevet Brigadier General (rank from May 30, 1848) for Mexican War service.
- 1849: Major Alfred Mordecai's elaborate study of European ordnance completed under Talcott's supervision; resulted in first comprehensive American field artillery system.
- Summer 1851: court-martialed at direction of Sec. of War Charles Conrad following dispute over munitions contracts to a Southern contractor — Bvt Col. Benjamin Huger had proceeded with the contract without Talcott's full knowledge or approval. Despite long-standing departmental policy authorizing Talcott to make open purchases at his discretion, he was dismissed from the Army. Huger was never charged but admonished by President Fillmore. Senate efforts to investigate or provide financial relief: failed.

narrative_blocks.header: Third Chief of Ordnance of the U.S. Army
narrative_blocks.body: George Talcott rose through the Ordnance Corps from 1813 lieutenant to 1848 Chief of Ordnance, supervising the major Ordnance Department expansion before and during the Mexican-American War. He received Brevet Brigadier General in 1849 for his Mexican War service and oversaw the 1849 Mordecai report that established the first comprehensive American field artillery system.

narrative_blocks.header: Court-martialed and dismissed in 1851 over munitions contracts
narrative_blocks.body: In summer 1851, Secretary of War Charles Conrad court-martialed Talcott over munitions contract awards to a Southern contractor — though Bvt. Col. Benjamin Huger had proceeded with the contract without Talcott's full knowledge or approval. Many felt the verdict unwarranted; Senate efforts to investigate or provide financial relief failed.

narrative_blocks.header: Farmed the same land his ancestor had broken
narrative_blocks.body: Jared G. Talcott and his son Charles H. occupied and farmed the Glastonbury homestead that had descended to him from his ancestor Deacon Benjamin Talcott who built the original house there in 1699. As of 1876 the land had been in the family for over 175 years — still bearing the bullet marks from Indian attacks on the fortified original house.

cross_connections.display_label: He farmed the same Glastonbury land Deacon Benjamin built on in 1699.

burial.plot_notes: Hollywood Cemetery, Richmond VA presumed (same as wife and son Charles Sr); not yet confirmed.

parents.father_research_notes: George Talcott Sr. (1755-1813), farmer at the Glastenbury homestead descended from Benjamin Talcott. Served briefly in the Revolutionary War.
parents.mother_research_notes: Abigail Goodrich (1767-1854), daughter of John and Abigail Deming Goodrich, m. George Sr. Feb 9, 1786 as his second wife after Vienna Bradford's death. Andrew was her youngest child. I-prefix entry pending.

sources.short: Findagrave (Lucia Hollerith biographical material citing Andrew Talcott 1797-1883)

narrative_blocks.header: From West Point to Maximilian's imperial railway
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated USMA 1818 second in his class, oversaw construction of Forts Adams, Monroe, Calhoun, and Hamilton, then surveyed the Ohio-Michigan border alongside Lt. Robert E. Lee. Late in life he engineered the steepest railway built up to that time — Veracruz to Mexico City for Maximilian's regime.

narrative_blocks.header: Stayed neutral, jailed as a Confederate spy anyway
narrative_blocks.body: Though his son served on Lee's staff and Lee called his wife 'the Beautiful Talcott,' he refused to fight for either side and went back to Mexico. Returning briefly to New York for railway supplies in March 1863, he was arrested as a CSA spy and held at Forts Lafayette and Warren before Gen. Dix released him.

narrative_blocks.header: The Horrebow-Talcott latitude method bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: In 1833 he extended a method by Danish astronomer Peder Horrebow for fixing latitude by observing zenith distances of stars on opposite sides of the zenith. The technique came to be called the Horrebow-Talcott method and was used by geodesists into the 20th century.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter married IBM founder Herman Hollerith
narrative_blocks.body: His granddaughter Lucia Beverly Talcott married Herman Hollerith on September 15, 1890. Hollerith's tabulating company became Computing-Tabulating-Recording in 1911 and was renamed IBM in 1924 — Andrew's descendants thus woven into the founding family of computing.

narrative_blocks.header: Lee called his wife 'the Beautiful Talcott'
narrative_blocks.body: He was Robert E. Lee's superior and friend at Hampton Roads from the 1820s, surveying the Ohio-Michigan border with him in 1835. Lee affectionately called his wife Harriet 'the Beautiful Talcott' for half a century.

narrative_blocks.header: His name is on a latitude method
narrative_blocks.body: Extending Peder Horrebow’s technique, Talcott’s 1833 refinement for fixing latitude from paired zenith stars became the Horrebow-Talcott Method, which still bears his name. The American Philosophical Society elected him in 1838.

narrative_blocks.header: Robert E. Lee worked under him
narrative_blocks.body: As superintending engineer at Forts Calhoun and Monroe on Hampton Roads he was superior and friend to the young Lee, surveying the Ohio-Michigan border with him in 1835 before resigning his captaincy for civil practice.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father-in-law whose Florida claim he sued to defend

education.institution: United States Military Academy at West Point
education.degree: Graduated July 24, 1818 — No. 2 in his class (No. 1 was Gen. Richard Delafield)
education.notes: Cadet from March 14, 1815 to July 24, 1818. Classmates included Gen. Harvey Brown, Major Ringold, and Lt. Col. Hartman Bache.

career.notes: First post after Army resignation. Specifically western division of the line.
Worked alongside young A. B. (Andrew) Gray.
Member of the commission for exploration and survey of the north-east boundary.
Examining Portsmouth and Pensacola navy yards and projecting stone and floating docks.
Jan 11, 1848 - July 31, 1855. Later appointed general manager. Note: was considered for Superintendent of the Coast Survey 1843 but the post went to Alexander Bache.

career.organization: Ohio and Mississippi Railroad (Cincinnati to St. Louis)
Mexico and Pacific Railway (Vera Cruz - Mexico City - Pacific)
career.notes: From Dec 1, 1857. Engaged by A. Escandon (with English financing) — supervised W. W. Finney of the Pony Express. Andrew was assigned the more difficult southern passage (rising 211 ft/mile over 23 miles, with the 380-ft Metlac River iron bridge — at the time the steepest railway ever undertaken). Suspended 1861-1862 when Civil War began (he became Virginia State Engineer Mar 1861 - Dec 1861); resumed under French-reorganized concession late 1860s under Maximilian until Juárez defeated Maximilian 1867.

career.role: Chief Engineer of the State of Virginia
career.organization: Commonwealth of Virginia (NOT Confederate States Army)
career.notes: Appointed March 1861 at request of Robert E. Lee; Colonel and State Engineer of Virginia. Charged with superintendence of river, coast, and harbor defenses of the State (Richmond and James River). Built/rebuilt Fort Boykin (star-shaped) and Fort Huger — both later crippled by ironclad USS Galena. Returned to Mexico when CSA government assumed the work in December 1861.

career.organization: Bon Air, Chesterfield County, Virginia (with son TMR Talcott)
career.notes: After returning from Mexico in 1867, with his son Col. Thomas Mann Randolph Talcott (T01842) invested in development of the Bon Air, VA community south of Richmond.

awards.name: Member, American Philosophical Society
awards.notes: Elected 1838 in recognition of his engineering and surveying contributions, particularly the Horrebow-Talcott Method of latitude determination.

military_service.war: Pre-Civil-War U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (peacetime)
military_service.unit: Corps of Engineers (Brevet 2nd Lt 1818 → 2nd Lt 1818 → 1st Lt 1820 → Capt 1830 → resigned 1836)
military_service.notes: Promoted Brevet 2nd Lt CoE July 24, 1818 (graduation day). Asst Engineer at Fort at Rouse's Point, NY 1818-19. 2nd Lt CoE Aug 14, 1818. Aide-de-camp to Bvt. Brig. Gen. Atkinson + Engineer on expedition establishing forts on upper Missouri and Yellowstone rivers Nov 1, 1820 - April 30, 1821. 1st Lt CoE Oct 1, 1820. Asst Engineer at defenses of Hampton Roads VA 1821-24. Engineer in charge of preliminary operations for Brenton's Point (Fort Adams RI), New Utrecht Point (Fort Hamilton NY) 1824-25; Fort Delaware DE 1825-26; Dismal Swamp Canal 1826-28; Fort Monroe 1828-34; Fort Calhoun 1828-35 (Hampton Roads VA). Brevet Captain Oct 1, 1830 for ten years faithful service. Astronomer for Ohio-Michigan boundary determination Dec 1, 1832 - May 30, 1836 (surveyed alongside then-Lt. Robert E. Lee in spring 1835). Captain CoE Dec 22, 1830. Superintending Engineer for Hudson River improvement NY 1834-36. Resigned September 21, 1836.

bio.bio_blurb: Talcott pioneer settler at Owego, New York

narrative_blocks.header: He led Yale students against the British raid
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale student during the Revolution, George Welles commanded a band of fellow students who volunteered to oppose the British invasion of New Haven in 1779. He graduated in the class of 1779.

narrative_blocks.header: Land agent and innkeeper on the Pennsylvania frontier
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale, Welles settled at Athens on the Pennsylvania frontier as agent for the Carroll lands and proprietor of the Athens Hotel. He grew rich in valuable lands and served as a justice of the peace.

narrative_blocks.header: He died in the Sheshequin fever epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: In 1813 a deadly epidemic the settlers called the 'Sheshequin fever' swept Tioga Point, striking only adults and sparing no one under fifteen. George Welles was among its victims, buried in late June at the height of the outbreak.

bio.bio_blurb: Yale-educated Pennsylvania land agent and innkeeper

narrative_blocks.header: The general who wrote Pennsylvania's Academy Bill
narrative_blocks.body: Elected to the Pennsylvania House in 1809 and the state Senate from 1815 to 1819, Henry Welles authored the 1813 Academy Bill to expand schooling across the commonwealth. Governor Simon Snyder named him an aide with the rank of general, the title by which everyone afterward knew him.

narrative_blocks.header: A township was named in his honor
narrative_blocks.body: A fluent orator said to charm any room or legislature he entered, Welles became one of the foremost makers of Athens, Pennsylvania. Wells Township was named for him.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife died weeks after the wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Henry married Phoebe Patrick in February 1809, but she died only weeks later, leaving him a young widower. Three years on he married Sarah 'Sally' Spalding, who would outlive him by forty-four years.

bio.photo_notes: Gen. Henry Welles (1781–1833), Pennsylvania state senator.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Welleses wed two Hollenbacks
narrative_blocks.body: Susan Welles married John Hollenback in 1805, and her brother Charles Fisher Welles later married John's sister Ellen, joining two of Judge Matthias Hollenback's children to two of George Welles's. Susan and John had no children of their own.

narrative_blocks.header: Bradford County's first clerk of courts
narrative_blocks.body: At twenty-three Charles Fisher Welles was appointed the first prothonotary and clerk of the courts of Bradford County, holding the offices six years. His court records were long admired as models of neatness and accuracy.

narrative_blocks.header: A diffident poet with a trenchant pen
narrative_blocks.body: A lifelong lover of poetry who studied law but was too shy to practice it, Welles instead wielded a busy pen for the press. His political essays were said to carry conviction to the reader and scathing rebuke to the corrupt politician.

narrative_blocks.header: A fall from his wagon killed him
narrative_blocks.body: Welles kept a hand in public affairs for half a century and rarely missed a term of court at Towanda. In 1866, getting down from his wagon, he was thrown heavily and died of his injuries.

bio.photo_notes: Charles Fisher Welles (1789–1866), first prothonotary of Bradford County.
bio.bio_blurb: Bradford County's first prothonotary and clerk

narrative_blocks.header: She preserved the family's Woodbridge legend
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Welles Pumpelly handed down the cherished story of her great-great-grandmother Jerusha Pitkin, the English lady whom the gentlemen of Hartford schemed to keep in the colony. Through her telling the tale of the ancestress of the Woodbridges was preserved as authentic family history.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

will.summary: Will of Lt. Benjamin Talcott, Glastonbury CT. Wife: £80 + use of parlor, cellar, garret, ovens, gardens, one-third of orchard and land west of highway to great river — during widowhood only. Son Benjamin: lands in Bolton + 124 acres Hebron (bought of Joseph and John Phelps). Son John: 300 acres Hebron (west of Jacob Root), 50 acres Hebron (north of Richard Curtis), one yoke of steers, £10 moveable, + £15 from Elizur. Son Samuel: south half of home lot, 153 acres beyond Black Ledge River (Glastonbury/Hebron border), half undivided Glastonbury lands, smith-shop with all smithery tools, longest gun, half team. Son Elizur: north half of home lot with all buildings (except smith-shop), 60 acres Ash Swamp Glastonbury, 96 acres near bare hill Glastonbury, other half undivided lands, newest gun, half team — he to pay John £15. Daughter Sarah: £100. Daughter Hannah: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Daughter Mehitabel: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Executors: son-in-law Jonathan Hale and son Benjamin Talcott. Witnesses: Samuel Smith Jr., Nathaniel Talcott, Thomas Wells. Estate inventory: £2563 15s. 8d.
will.note: Will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.106-109.

cross_connections.display_label: Glastonbury homestead passed intact to him 175 years later.

narrative_blocks.header: His house bore bullet marks until 1854
narrative_blocks.body: He built a fortified wooden house on Main Street, Glastonbury in 1699 — a refuge from Indian attacks for the surrounding community. The house bore bullet marks until it was taken down soon after 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Sarah died in childbed at thirty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister, granddaughter of original 1636 Hartford settler John Hollister, married him in 1699 and bore him eight children. She died in childbirth at the homestead October 15, 1715.

narrative_blocks.header: Sons signed Bolton's 1731 founding patent
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons Benjamin and Nathaniel were listed as Bolton Proprietors in the 1731 patent, signed by their cousin Governor Joseph Talcott. A third son Elizur later chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Wright cousins married two Talcott siblings
narrative_blocks.body: His son Elizur married Ruth Wright; his daughter Mehitabel married Ruth's first cousin Hezekiah Wright. Two Wright cousins thus married two Talcott siblings, weaving the families together.

narrative_blocks.header: Farm in family from 1643 through 1876
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather John Talcott had bought the Glastonbury farm from Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve in 1643. The land was still owned by descendant Jared G. Talcott in 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in childbed; the baby followed
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister married Deacon Benjamin Talcott and died in childbed at Glastonbury in 1715, at thirty-eight. The daughter whose birth she did not survive, Abigail, lived only eighteen days more.

political_offices.title: Deputy to General Court
political_offices.notes: Secretary of the General Court in absence of Col. Allyn during October session 1684

political_offices.title: Member, Standing Committee
political_offices.notes: Appointed to order measures in intervals of General Court

political_offices.title: Captain of the Troop, Hartford County
Assistant
political_offices.notes: From 1683 except during Andros' administration, was Assistant until his death

will.summary: Will of Captain Samuel Talcott, Wethersfield CT. Bequeathed to sons: Samuel (home lot south, great meadow parcels, Westfield lot); John (home lot north, 5 acres great meadow, 12 acres Westfield); Elizur (Westfield brook land, 6 acres meadow, 50-acre lot near Farmington line); Joseph (30 acres Westfield); Benjamin (north half, east side Connecticut River, undivided Indian lands, south half 200-acre court grant); Nathaniel (south half Nawburk lot, undivided east-river land, north half 200-acre grant, one cow). Daughters: Hannah Chester £90; Rachel £80. Wife Mary: £10/yr, use of lower room, garden, one cow during widowhood. Executor: son Samuel Talcott. Overseers: Capt. Robert Wells, Ensign Samuel Butler.
will.note: Original will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.97-102.

T00036.notable_legacy: "From these two sons, John and Samuel, are descended all of the name of TALCOTT in America." — Talcott Pedigree (1876). All living Talcotts in America descend from Capt. Samuel and/or his brother John (T00035).

cross_connections.display_label: founded Hartford; lines joined via Hannah Holyoke
Capt. Samuel Talcott's wife Hannah Holyoke was the daughter of.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard College 1658
narrative_blocks.body: Harvard College 1658 — the scholar of the Talcott family; his father bequeathed all his books to Samuel (except the 'Martyr Book' given to John)

narrative_blocks.header: One of the original proprietors of the town
narrative_blocks.body: One of the original proprietors of the town of Glastonbury, CT — owned the lot purchased by his father John in 1643 of Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve, 44 rods wide running three miles to the wilderness; still owned by descendants at time of writing

narrative_blocks.header: Captain of the Troop, Hartford County 1681
narrative_blocks.body: Captain of the Troop, Hartford County 1681; Assistant of the Colony 1683-1691

narrative_blocks.header: From John and Samuel Talcott are descended ALL
narrative_blocks.body: From John and Samuel Talcott are descended ALL of the name Talcott in America

narrative_blocks.header: Direct ancestor of the compiler Samuel Talcott Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Direct ancestor of the compiler Samuel Talcott Hooker through the maternal Talcott line

military_service.notes: Rose through Trained Band ranks during this period

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Sarah married Deacon Benjamin Talcott in 1698
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Sarah Hollister married Deacon Benjamin Talcott on 5 January 1698 — the marriage that connected his Wethersfield Hollister line to the Hartford Talcott line. That single match brings his descent down through the Talcott branch of this project.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived wife Sarah by eleven years at Glastonbury
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Goodrich died in 1700 after thirty-three years of marriage and nine children. He lived eleven more years and died at Glastonbury CT on November 24, 1711. Their daughter Sarah had married Deacon Benjamin Talcott just two years before her mother's death.

crossConnections.display_label: Hooker descendant served in his Troop of horses, 1757 alarm.
